transcript.text: Ooh, there's some connections through our investors.
think there's been companies that have worked This meeting is being recorded.
And then they referred me to Marcel, so I just pinged him.
That was the extent of the conversation.
Okay, sounds good.
Do you have high-level context on what we do here at GrowthX?
Very high level.
But basically the source of it is I do want to improve our GEO and where we rank on particular queries, especially around parallel dialing, which is sort of the core of our current product.
Yeah, and as I understand it, it's a GrowthX is a way to help improve that, understand where there's gaps, generate content that's going to help for GEO and SEO.
Yeah.
I mean, think of us, Peter, as...
Like an end-to-end partner, right?
So agency is like the simplest way to put it, but we've got an internal platform.
We're working on bringing our clients into the platform as well.
Really a combination of like sophisticated AI workflows and then experts in the loop so that we can help you scale out, you know, quality answers to your ICP's most important questions, right?
With AI search, you need like a much larger surface area of content.
Just being familiar with Nooks, I was kind of digging in and, you know, like 98% of the traffic I think is branded.
And I think it's like a huge opportunity right on the non-branded side, given that when someone knows about Nooks, like myself, they turn into fans pretty quickly.
And digging into your blog, it just doesn't look like you guys have created a ton of content, right?
There's some great editorial pieces out there, but is it really like an opportunity to scale the content creation efforts that...
I mean, obviously, like, you know, showing up in AI search, everybody wants to do that, but is it really just being able to scale quality that the team needs some help with?
Yeah, yeah, I mean, we're a small team, so outsourcing key things like this is definitely an option for us.
Sounds good.
Do you work with any agency partners around SEO or around content creation today?
No, no, we don't.
And then how big is the team in-house that creates content, just kind of getting the lay of the land?
So there's five people in total, and probably our biggest content creator is probably me and our product marketer, but neither of us, it's not like our full-time job.
We did have someone, but they actually were departed, unfortunately.
Understood.
And are you and the team, like, leveraging AI as part of the content creation efforts today?
Yes, we are, yeah.
How far along in that journey, like, would you say you are?
Yes,
Just using kind of custom GPTs or more advanced than that?
Yeah, I don't know that it's particularly advanced.
mean, it's, you know, trying to understand, well, what are the gaps in our content?
You know, we produced a few more pieces just recently that are in the queue to get put up.
So we've got like five pieces that should be going up this week.
Yeah, so it's basically like querying to understand what the gap is, querying to understand what the prioritization should be for the content, and then building the content, editing it, and posting.
Awesome, awesome.
What I can do today, I can go through like a quick presentation on GrowthX to give you better context.
I'll go into some of our customer content examples, and then I can actually jump into the platform and show you how the AI workflows and the humans are involved in the process.
Does that sound good to you?
Yeah, that's great.
Awesome, and feel free to interrupt me as I go.
Definitely.
You know, want to make this as relevant as possible for you.
I think, you know, part of our secret sauce is really like Marcel built growthx as the solution he always wanted as a CMO.
He's always believed brands should be publishers.
He never was one to spend much on paid channels in his career.

And so, you know, whether it was IBM where he led content marketing and stood up this microsite called securityintelligence.com or scale.ai, where he was CMO heading into the acquisition by Meta, and then Deepgram, where he, you know, went through this same process of building an organic content engine, but now using AI workflows for each step of the process.
It's really just this idea that, you know, Marcel is building something that he built with a huge team at every other stop in his journey, right?
And so, you know, where can AI automate some of the process without sacrificing quality?
It can automate the process.
It generating a great brief around a content assignment.
It can automate the process of doing deep research and formulating an outline, even coming up with initial draft.
And then it can also automate the process of fact checking the content.
And that's a huge piece, I would say, right?
Because everybody is skeptical of AI hallucinating.
It's not enough just to kind of run things through as one shot.
You have to really break it down into first principles and have a very step-by-step process that reinforces itself.
And so a lot of what we do is we separate our engagements into a strategy sprint and then a growth execution.

So that when it comes to actually creating content, we've done all the context engineering up front so that we can make sure the workflows are being fed with artifacts around your audience, personas, or ICP, your company context, your brand voice.
And then because we've built, you know, our own platform, we
Customize the workflows and customize the inputs from clients to client.
And so what's great about partnering with GrowthX is we free up a lot of bandwidth for your teams.
We are going to handle everything from identifying the opportunities to actually going in and staging and publishing in your CMS.
And then we'll deliver all of the LLM visibility and monitoring so that you can see exactly where you're showing up.
And we can track that on a week-by-week, month-by-month cadence.
But any questions, high-level, just about kind of the difference to who we are at GrowthX and the process that we go through, I'll continue to expand on this.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Cool.
And so the platform we'll go into today, but just to give you an idea of the support on the resourcing side, each client has a managing director, a managing editor, and a content manager.
And these folks all come from SEO backgrounds, content marketing backgrounds, our chief content officer.
He was the editor-in-chief at TechCrunch for over a decade, and so we really are trying to bring the best of both together.

And so, you know, like I mentioned, I was digging into the opportunity, right, on SEMrush with Nooks and saw that, you know, most of the traffic is branded, and there is an opportunity to increase non-branded traffic.
There's also an opportunity to scale out the content, as we discussed, right?
You guys have something like 100 organic pages to date.
If go into just the blog pages, you've got like 67 different blog pieces.
We typically start off on editorial, so net new and refresh for the blog, but also think like guides or comparison pieces.
And then we can look at more programmatic opportunities.
So, you know, Ramp is a good example of a client who we've done some interesting programmatic use cases.
They've got all this internal data around spend, and they want to be the authority.
When it comes to business spending.
And so what we did here was we created this entire vendor directory.
And every single page, we populated these interactive charts with their own spend data.
And so now, you know, if someone is, you know, going in and looking up information around Airtable, Ramp starts to show up.
Or if they're looking at, you know, maybe alternatives or comparisons from one tool to the next, we're really able to scale out the content through the process, you know, that our team goes through.
When you're thinking about the opportunity for Nooks, were you thinking just in terms of, you know, editorial content?
Were you thinking about other use cases?
I mean, editorial content was probably the first thing I was thinking of.
Yeah, yeah.
I mean, this is interesting, though.
I, I, um, yeah, I was just thinking about a previous company I worked at, how they, they.
Would have loved this, because they do a lot of these kind of comparisons.
But I'd have to think about how it would work for something like this would work for Nooks, or what the match would be for Nooks.
Yeah, when it comes to how we actually identify the opportunities themselves, what we'll do is, you know, we'll do like our own deep research.
We'll ingest resources that you have around your ICP positioning, messaging, brand documents.
We can take, you a sales deck or transcripts.
And this allows us to create artifacts pretty quickly.
But we'll also do like a kickoff call with the content team, product deep dive with that product marketer.
And then we'll start to look at the opportunity against, you know, your main competitors.

So that when it comes to, you know, generating assignments, we're going to do that within the realm of the search volume, the keyword difficulty, the competitor content, who would you say, like, you guys' top two?
Who are three biggest competitors are today?
Aurum would be the number one, O-R-U-M.
Aurum, and then who would be number two, would you say?
There's a bit of a gap between the first competitor and the second competitor.
So probably Reggie, R-E-G-I-E.ai.
Okay, yeah, I've heard of Reggie.
Aurum, I haven't even heard, I mean, I've heard the name, but I don't know.
guess the experience, at Seros, the experience with Nooks was so good that nobody even thought about anybody else.
That was good.
Yeah, exactly, exactly.
And so, you know, what we'll do here is we'll really start to generate these artifacts.
And as an example, like, Engine is in the business travel space.
We also work with Outreach, more relevant company in your space.
And Engine, you know, they wanted to create all these different business travel guides for different industries.

And so jumping into the platform, you can see that we've got, you know, these artifacts, a knowledge base, the different, you know, opportunities that we're working on, which we manage this in Airtable, but it's, you know, coming into the platform over the next month or two.
And then these pipelines where the workflows can refresh content or generate new content.

And so for Engine, what's nice about the artifacts that we created for them, and again, this can be different from client to client, just depending on what is most relevant and helpful for you, is we verticalized these different personas artifacts.
And so there's some information around, you know, why Engine, what differentiates their platform for booking travel.
And then there's these three distinct personas, where when you think about AI search, right, you can just query in so many different ways.
So you kind of have to embrace the long tail and almost like get in the mind.
And so what we're doing here is we're looking at, you know, for this construction operations manager, what are their fears and uncertainties, motivations?
How do you win or lose their trust?
What's their biggest pain points?
Why would they choose their platform?

And so this combined with the analysis that we do into the opportunity and the topic clusters that we establish where you want to have authority, that informs the different assignments that we create.

And then from there, we're just able to scale that process by creating content drafts and having your team in a position where all you need to do is review, provide feedback, and approve so that we can get to a place where we're publishing daily is what we actually strive for.
So five articles per week is what we would publish or refresh on your CMS.
Got it.
Okay.
And so I mentioned, right, like,
We split this up into two different phases.
In the first phase, we'll have the first pieces of content created around week four.
And then you'll have about probably 10 to 20 published articles by the end of the phase one strategy sprint.
We do this so that you have this ability to basically opt out if you're not feeling confident in the roadmap that we're going to tackle or the quality that we can reach.
And then in phase two, we're in a place where we can move to that scale.
And again, we target five pieces per week.
And what's nice about how we work is that, let's say, we've done a bunch of editorial content, and then we've identified this programmatic opportunity that we can move into.
We can just swap out editorial for programmatic, right?
You don't have to ever think about the 15K increasing.
The only way that it would increase is if you wanted to do, right, the five editorial per week and programmatic at the same time.
Or maybe you wanted to.

So we can work on different custom work streams is what we call them, but I would say that the ones we do for every single client we work with would be editorial and then programmatic if there's an opportunity, and then from there, we can get more custom if there's different content use cases that you're interested in having us execute.
Okay.

And so tools and calculators is like another work stream we've created for ramp this per diem calculator, and we built right at the interactive map that you see there is this configuration where like someone could go in and insert dates and choose a specific county and city, but we've also got programmatic pages for every city in America, so that if someone comes in and says what's the average per diem in Houston.
Right ramp has a page that answers that question.
question.

And what we're doing is we're just optimizing all the text on the page to be SEO and GEO friendly so that, you know, again, you can scale quality without having to have a team internally create all this content manually or like using AI tools.
But then maybe the time to value is a lot slower.
You have to refactor the workflows as models change, things of that nature.
So these like these kind of guides that are like interactive, how like that's that's just one content piece.
It's not like it's like one to one with when you say there's five content pieces per week, this this is one of those five.

I would say, I would say this one is more of like a programmatic use case where for the first right month, we're going to be actually setting up the pipeline and setting up the workflow to be able to execute at scale.
For editorial, you know, there's like no design work really involved there because we're piggybacking.
On the blog template that you've got in your CMS, right, and handling the text creation, cross-linking, the actual, you know, content itself.
In a situation like this, it would be more like, you know, we'd look at making sure that we've got the right inputs so that we can, the right data inputs so that we can scale.
And then when it comes to the quantity that we can scale at, you know, once we've got the infrastructure set up, we could do, right, 25 pieces per week.
could do 100 pieces per week.
With programmatic, you know, as you probably know, right, it's really more of a matter of having the inputs right and then having the template ready to go.
From there, you know, you can really create pages at a massive scale.
But you want to start with the editorial foundation.
Okay.
Got it.
And so just an idea of, like, the different types of content we can create.
We've created this expense classifier where people...
We can see the expense category for different expenses.
We've created integrations pages for Webflow.
And what's nice about some of these integrations pages is that we get into, like, the use cases, what you can build, frequently asked questions.
There's, like, a code snippet built in that shows how someone would tactically go in and create the integration.
And then we've also created things like these guides for augment code.
So, augment code, you know, they wanted to compare our content to their blog.
And we've, like, 3X the traffic of their blog, got, like, 300 signups per week.
And a lot of this is thought leadership.

So it's, you know, tool comparisons, but also getting deeper into, like, the AI coding space and augment, you know, being more of an authority around where this space is going as it's something that is, you know, continuing to unfold.
Similar to, right, like, Nook's and AI-powered dialers.
It's, it's...
Something that you can have thought leadership around, because a lot of this is changing, right, on like a week by week or month by month basis.
Okay.
So tell me more about, like, with the, with the GPTs, like, and the answers there.
Like, how do you work with them on finding what are the top queries and then developing content with that in mind?
Yeah.
So we use a partner called Scrunch for a lot of the LLM analytics.
And so what you can do in Scrunch is you can track all these different prompts and you can do it by basically setting up different topics that you want to be able to go more granular around.
So for augment code, and there's a lot of different topics that we track here, you know, they've got AI developer tools, and then there's 1600 different prompts, collaborative coding platforms, 200.
And so you can actually go in and look at specific prompts here, but we're not, like, partnering with ChatGPT, right?
We're leveraging their APIs to bring that data in, and then you have to decide, like, which topics do you want to track?
And then from there, you can have a bunch of prompts that you're tracking within those topics.

But, right, when you think about someone searching with ChatGPT or something similar, you know, it'd be impossible to find every single prompt because everybody is going to say something a little different, right, as opposed to just software for X, Y, Z.
Does that help answer your question there?
I know it's a bit of a tricky question to answer.
Yeah, no, I get it.
And so the workflows themselves, just to kind of go into the augment code space, right, the artifacts are feeding all the content creation, but we can also see.
We up a knowledge base so that we can store the research that we're doing and then optimize for retrieval.
And so, you know, we've got all of this more technical information related to augment code.
And then anytime we're creating a new piece of content, we're making sure that that research is stored in a knowledge graph so that we can optimize for retrieval.
Right.
We're going to semantically be able to pull in that research as we create a new piece.
And to give you an idea of what's happening with the workflows themselves, how our team is involved in the process.
And so this example here is like creating an assignment.
And so what we're doing is we're going in and we're giving some direction.
We've got a topic that we want to write about, and then we've got those artifacts that are feeding in.
And what you have as the output is this really robust brief around the opportunity for the assignment.
But in the background, you know, there's a lot of things happening in tandem.
So...
So...

We're going in and looking at the keyword traffic, the organic results, the SERP ranking factors, the competitor content, the search intent, and then all of that, all these different steps in the process that ran for about 10 minutes, that is what leads to the output that you see here, which is this brief.
And then when you take it through the process of actually creating a specific article, what's nice about our workflows here is that they're agentic.
So they'll run in a loop until we hit a certain score.
And if you looked at the research, for example, in the research step, what we're doing is we're generating a plan, we're executing research questions, we're synthesizing those results.
And then if it identifies gaps and doesn't hit a certain threshold, it'll run that research again, focused on the specific gaps.
And so, you know, in this example, right, there's a ton of direction that we're giving.
We've got those different artifacts around the persona.
for augment code and who they want to write for.
We've got additional context around what augment code means to those personas.

And then we're actually using that to fine tune the research to be like exploratory, hybrid, strict, but it's focused on researching for a specific audience, satisfying the research toward an objective, answering questions with success criteria or source requirements.
And when it's synthesizing the results, what's going to happen is it's going to tell us why it didn't need a certain score.

And then again, rerun the research focused on solving for those gaps, generating additional research questions, and then storing that research so that we can build upon what we're doing as we create a specific assignment for them.
And so, you know, every step in this process, it can be customized from client to client.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay.
We could bring in, right, your specific writing guidelines and even generate images or infographics.
And the idea is that you leverage the best of all the most popular models out there, right?

You're not just stuck with Claude or ChatGPT, but we're able to leverage all of these different models, update these workflows as the models change, and then have custom steps in the process, including, right, as an article is generated, being able to go and take every single passage from that article and do more deep research, validate, right, that the research is accurate, and if it's not, suggest a rewrite, and our team is in the process so that they can, right, make changes to the content or even give instructions, you know, add three bullet points at the end.
Of this introduction.

And just speed up the process of being able to scale quality for your team and just do it in a way that, you know, you couldn't do if you were just using, like, ChatGPT to try to create content without as much context as we feed it.
Is this helpful?
I want to make sure I'm giving you, like, a good look under the hood and all the moving parts.
Yeah, no, it's very helpful.
I, I feel like it could be very interesting.

I think the challenge I'm going to have at the moment is we're doing some very important, like, positioning work that's going to, like, very much affect what all the artifacts are that everything is based on.
Um, so, um, so it's probably, like, it's going to be dependent on that.
And that's going to be like six to eight weeks for, for the work on that.
Um, so I wonder whether I need to put this under my cap and kind of stay close to it, but we, but we do need to take some.
Some of these important steps before we start generating content that may be aligned or not.
Yeah, are you guys, I guess, how much are you changing your ICP?
Is it?
Yeah, the ICP is not going to radically change, no.
To what, I guess, to what extent are things changing?
Like, could you maybe just give me a little more context to help me understand whether the, you know, what timing could make sense if it does?
Yeah, yeah.
So we are defining what our platform story is, how to group the functions within that story, what are the key messages for each product.
Yeah, so, I mean, it's not changing in terms of customers or ICP needs X solved.
Right.
But it is changing.
In terms of, this is how we would message how we do solve those problems.
That makes a lot of sense, right?

It's really just like, for the next fiscal year, for the next state of the product that you guys are building, you are rethinking the way you message some of your core features, differentiations, what the story behind why Nooks is, essentially.
Yes, yeah.
Yeah, think it could work out pretty decently in the sense that, you know, right now, we're booked through the end of the year.
We do two strategy sprint kickoffs per week, but we're definitely focused on, like, the best ICP fits.
I would say Nooks would be a great ICP fit because you guys have some domain authority.
The content isn't super technical.

We've been able to create technical content for a lot of clients, but as I'm sure you can imagine, there's a lot of intensive review with that, which can sometimes slow down how fast we generate results because you do need to make this.
It's a bit of a numbers game.
But if it's helpful, I'm happy to connect with another member of your team and give them an introduction or look to maybe bring Marcel to a follow-up conversation, whatever helps.
Okay.
Okay, great.
Well, yeah, maybe what I can do is as we get a little further along, we're still like it's early days with it, with that project.
But just let's stay in touch because I think this is important stuff.
And, yeah, I just want to see if this is the right path for us.
So this is a great introduction, and let's just keep in touch for the next couple weeks.
Sounds good, Peter.
I'll do some digging on the opportunity against Orem so that next conversation we can come in with a bit more tactical insights around Nooks versus Orem.
Okay, that sounds great.
Thanks, Tyler.
Good to meet you.
Have a great week.
You too.
Bye-bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Evaluate GrowthX's content scaling services for Nooks.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=15.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**The Opportunity:** Nooks' traffic is \~98% branded (from \~100 organic pages), leaving a large, untapped non-branded SEO opportunity, especially for its core "parallel dialing" product.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=86.0)
  - [**The Solution:** GrowthX offers an AI-powered agency model to scale content, targeting 5 articles/week. It uses agentic workflows (e.g., self-correcting research loops) and human editors to ensure quality and fact-check AI output.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=58.0)
  - [**The Blocker:** Nooks is 6–8 weeks into a major positioning project that will redefine its messaging. This work must be completed before engaging GrowthX to ensure all content is aligned with the new brand story.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1468.0)
  - [**The Path Forward:** Engagement is paused. Tyler will research the Nooks vs. Aurum competitive landscape to provide tactical insights for a follow-up meeting in a few weeks.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1658.0)

## Topics

### The Problem: Nooks' Content Gap

  - [**Goal:** Improve non-branded SEO ranking for core queries like "parallel dialing."](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=21.0)
  - [**Current State:**](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=86.0)
      - [Traffic is \~98% branded, indicating a large, untapped market.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=86.0)
      - [Content output is limited (\~100 organic pages, 67 blog posts) due to a small team (5 people, no dedicated content role).](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=146.0)
      - [Current AI use is basic (e.g., custom GPTs for topic ideation).](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=169.0)

### The Solution: GrowthX's AI-Powered Agency

  - [**Model:** An end-to-end partner combining an internal AI platform with human experts.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=58.0)
  - [**Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=776.0)
    1.  [**Strategy Sprint (Phase 1):** A 1-month trial to build a content roadmap and publish 10–20 articles. This de-risks the engagement and allows Nooks to opt out if quality or strategy is misaligned.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=776.0)
    2.  [**Growth Execution (Phase 2):** Scale content to 5 articles/week.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=806.0)
  - [**Pricing:** A single flat fee covers one "workstream" (e.g., editorial, programmatic). Adding a second workstream incurs an additional fee.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=815.0)
  - [**Team:** Each client gets a Managing Director, Managing Editor, and Content Manager.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=402.0)

### How GrowthX's Platform Works

  - [**Artifacts:** Custom knowledge bases built from client documents (ICP, brand voice, sales decks) to guide AI workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=553.0)
  - [**Agentic Workflows:** AI agents run in loops, self-correcting until a quality score is met.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1280.0)
      - [**Example:** The research agent identifies gaps, reruns research to fill them, and stores findings in a knowledge graph for future retrieval.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1280.0)
  - [**Fact-Checking:** A dedicated workflow validates every passage against sources to prevent AI hallucinations.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1394.0)
  - [**Human Oversight:** Editors provide instructions and make final adjustments to drafts.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1394.0)

### The Blocker: Nooks' Positioning Project

  - [**Project Scope:** A 6–8 week initiative to define the "platform story," group functions, and establish key messages for each product.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1468.0)
  - [**Impact:** The project will redefine *how* Nooks solves problems, making it a prerequisite for any content strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1517.0)
  - [**Decision:** Engagement is paused. GrowthX's services are a strong fit, but the timing is wrong until Nooks' new messaging is finalized.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1468.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Peter:** Re-engage with GrowthX in a few weeks once the positioning project is further along.](https://fathom.video/share/oJPVJXzFjaEho7qDxsPtAGkVtR-fV5zh?tab=summary&timestamp=1635.0)
